title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Saving the Reef: The Battle for Coral
content.text: Beneath the surface of the tropical ocean lies an ecosystem of staggering complexity. Coral reefs cover less than one percent of the ocean floor, yet they support an estimated twenty-five percent of all marine species. They are also among the most threatened ecosystems on Earth. (37) [37] Scientists estimate that half of the world's coral reefs have already been lost since the 1950s, and projections for the coming decades are alarming. [/37] || The primary threat is climate change. When sea temperatures rise even one or two degrees above normal for extended periods, coral polyps expel the algae that live within their tissues and give them their colour and much of their energy. (38) [38] This process, known as bleaching, can be reversed if temperatures return to normal quickly enough, but repeated or prolonged bleaching events cause the coral to die. [/38] || Ocean acidification presents a second, distinct threat. As the ocean absorbs more carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, the water becomes more acidic, which inhibits the ability of coral to build their calcium carbonate skeletons. (39) [39] Even reefs that survive bleaching events face an increasingly hostile chemical environment. [/39] || In response, conservation scientists have developed a range of intervention strategies. Coral gardening — cultivating fragments of coral in underwater nurseries and then transplanting them onto degraded reefs — has shown promising results in several countries. (40) [40] Selective breeding programmes aim to identify and propagate coral varieties with greater heat tolerance. [/40] || Some researchers are exploring more radical solutions. 'Assisted evolution' involves deliberately exposing coral to warmer conditions to accelerate the natural selection of heat-resistant strains. (41) [41] Critics worry that manipulating the genetic composition of wild populations could have unforeseen ecological consequences. [/41] || The fate of coral reefs will ultimately depend on whether humanity can reduce carbon emissions quickly enough. (42) [42] Conservationists argue that while reef restoration buys time, it cannot substitute for addressing the root cause of the crisis. [/42]

paragraphs.A: Without algae, the coral turns white — giving the phenomenon the name 'coral bleaching'.
paragraphs.B: Other researchers focus on controlling populations of the crown-of-thorns starfish, which feeds voraciously on coral.
paragraphs.C: The combined effect of these chemical and thermal stresses makes recovery progressively less likely.
paragraphs.D: The ethical questions surrounding such interventions are the subject of intense debate.
paragraphs.E: No amount of local conservation work can compensate for the damage done by rising global temperatures.
paragraphs.F: Pollution, overfishing and coastal development add further pressure to already weakened reef systems.
paragraphs.G: The scale of the decline is a measure of how dramatically human activity has altered the ocean in a very short time.

questions.explanation: G señala que la escala del declive refleja cómo la actividad humana ha alterado el océano, apoyando las cifras alarmantes sobre la pérdida de arrecifes.
A describe que sin algas el coral se vuelve blanco, explicando el origen del nombre 'blanqueamiento de coral' mencionado a continuación.
C indica que el efecto combinado de ambas amenazas (térmica y química) hace la recuperación cada vez menos probable, sintetizando los dos párrafos anteriores.
B menciona el control de las poblaciones de la estrella de mar corona de espinas como otra estrategia de conservación, ampliando la lista de intervenciones.
D señala que las preguntas éticas sobre estas intervenciones son objeto de un intenso debate, apoyando las preocupaciones sobre la evolución asistida.
E afirma que ninguna cantidad de conservación local puede compensar el daño de las temperaturas globales en aumento, apoyando el argumento final sobre la necesidad de reducir emisiones.
